name: ungrammar
summary: Schema format used by rust-analyzer to describe the typed syntax-tree API generated on top of a lossless concrete syntax tree. Unlike a parser grammar, ungrammar specifies the AST/CST facade that tools consume: node variants, children, optional fields, and lists.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
ungrammar is a small schema language for describing the typed syntax-tree API that sits on top of a lossless concrete syntax tree. Its main lesson is architectural: the grammar that accepts text and the schema that tools consume do not have to be the same artifact. A parser may preserve every token and error node, while a generated typed facade exposes only meaningful nodes, fields, and lists.

The pattern is useful for any new language with formatter, refactoring, macro, or language-server ambitions: keep parsing lossless, then generate typed accessors that give tools a stable source-adjacent API.

sections.title: Schema example
sections.content:
A schema describes the tree API rather than the parse automaton. A tiny expression language might describe declarations and expressions like this:

```text
SourceFile = Item*
Item = Function | Struct
Function = Name ParamList Block
ParamList = Param*
Param = Name TypeRef
Expression = Literal | NameRef | BinaryExpression | CallExpression
BinaryExpression = lhs:Expression operator:Token rhs:Expression
CallExpression = callee:Expression ArgList
ArgList = Expression*
```

A generator can turn this into typed node wrappers (`Function`, `Expression`, `CallExpression`) over a lossless tree. The wrappers do not own the syntax; they interpret it. This lets the parser remain error-tolerant while tools get precise APIs such as `function.parameters()` or `call.arguments()`.

sections.title: Design lesson
sections.content: For a new language, the reusable design is to make syntax shape explicit and generated. Hand-written accessors drift from the grammar; schema-generated accessors make every tool consume the same tree contract. The trade-off is that the schema becomes an API compatibility surface: renaming a node or field is not an internal parser edit once formatters, refactorings, macros, and language-server features depend on it.
